children.name: Balearica
children.description: Crowned cranes — the most ancient crane lineage, distinguished by their stiff golden crest of wiry feathers atop the head and their ability to perch in trees, a behaviour unique among cranes. They inhabit African savanna wetlands and grasslands. The display of a crowned crane — bowing, leaping, and calling with its inflatable red throat pouch — is one of the most elaborate in the bird world.

children.name: Balearica pavonina
children.description: Found across the Sahel and Sudanian savanna from Senegal to Ethiopia. The golden crown of stiff feathers, white cheek patches, and dark grey body are distinctive. Vulnerable due to wetland drainage and capture for the wildlife trade.

children.name: Balearica regulorum
children.description: The national bird of Uganda, found in eastern and southern African wetlands. Distinguished from the black crowned crane by its larger cheek patch. Declining due to habitat loss and the pet trade; listed as Endangered.

children.name: Leucogeranus
children.description: The Siberian crane — a critically endangered, pure white crane that breeds in the Arctic tundra of eastern Siberia and winters in a single wetland in China (Poyang Lake). Its haunting, flute-like calls and spectacular migration route made it a cultural icon in Russia and China. The entire eastern population funnels through Poyang Lake, making it extremely vulnerable to habitat change. A separate central population migrating to India has been functionally extinct since 2002.

children.name: Leucogeranus leucogeranus
children.description: A spectacular all-white crane with black wingtips and a bare crimson face. The eastern population of approximately 4,000 birds winters at Poyang Lake in China. The species is Critically Endangered and culturally revered across Russia and Asia.

children.name: Grus
children.description: The true cranes — tall, long-legged birds of open wetlands and grasslands whose bugling calls and spectacular migration concentrations make them among the most iconic of all Palearctic birds. The common crane (trana) is Sweden's defining migratory spectacle: each April, over 100,000 cranes converge on Lake Hornborga in Västergötland to rest and refuel before continuing north to their breeding grounds, making it one of the greatest wildlife events in Europe. Cranes are among the longest-lived birds, forming pair bonds that last decades and engaging in elaborate dancing displays — leaping, wing-spreading, and bowing — that strengthen their partnership. In Sweden, the crane's resonant, bugling 'trrop trrop' call is so deeply embedded in the national consciousness that it has become synonymous with the arrival of spring.

children.name: Grus grus
children.description: The trana — Sweden's most beloved migratory bird. Breeds in bogs and swamp forests across Sweden from Skåne to Lapland, with a population of around 80,000 pairs. The Hornborga gathering each April draws over 20,000 human visitors and is one of Scandinavia's premier wildlife events. Cranes mate for life; pairs perform elaborate dancing ceremonies year-round to reinforce the bond. Their loud, trumpeting calls, produced by a convoluted trachea coiled inside the breastbone, carry for kilometres across open farmland.

children.name: Grus japonensis
children.description: The red-crowned crane, also called the Japanese crane and the Manchurian crane, is a large Northeast Asian crane among the rarest cranes in the world. In some parts of its range, it is known as a symbol of luck, longevity, and fidelity.

children.name: Grus vipio
children.description: A pale grey crane with a distinctive white nape and red patch around the eye. Breeds in the wetlands of northeastern Mongolia, China, and Siberia. Listed as Vulnerable due to habitat loss and wetland conversion in its East Asian wintering grounds.

children.name: Grus antigone
children.description: The tallest flying bird in the world, standing up to 176 cm. Found in India, Southeast Asia, and northern Australia. Sacred in India and culturally protected in many regions. Three subspecies are recognised, including the Australian form (G. a. gillae). Listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Grus canadensis
children.description: The most abundant crane species in the world, with approximately 650,000 individuals. Breeds across northern North America, Cuba, and eastern Siberia. The spectacular autumn gathering of 500,000 sandhill cranes along the Platte River in Nebraska is North America's greatest crane spectacle.

children.name: Grus monacha
children.description: A small, dark-grey crane with a white neck and head, named for its 'hooded' appearance. Breeds in the taiga bogs of central and eastern Siberia. Winters primarily in Japan and Korea. Listed as Vulnerable with fewer than 10,000 individuals.

children.name: Grus nigricollis
children.description: The only alpine crane in the world, breeding on the high Tibetan Plateau at elevations above 4,000 m. Its striking black neck and head contrast with a white body. Sacred in Tibetan and Bhutanese culture. Listed as Near Threatened with fewer than 10,000 individuals.

children.name: Grus rubicunda
children.description: An iconic Australian crane, found in wetlands and grasslands across northern and eastern Australia. The name comes from the Aboriginal Gamilaraay language. Famous for its elaborate dancing displays, which are the most acrobatic of any crane.

children.name: Grus americana
children.description: North America's tallest bird at 1.5 m and one of the most famous conservation success stories. Reduced to just 15 individuals in 1941, intensive captive breeding and reintroduction programmes brought the wild population back to around 500 birds. Known for its loud, single-note bugling call that carries for kilometres across its wetland breeding grounds. Listed as Endangered.

children.name: Anthropoides
children.description: Demoiselle cranes and blue cranes — elegant, relatively small cranes of dry steppes and semi-arid grasslands far removed from the wetland habitats favoured by most of their relatives. The demoiselle crane is the world's smallest crane, breeding across the central Asian steppes from the Black Sea to Mongolia and wintering in India. Its name comes from French — given by Marie Antoinette who was charmed by its dainty, graceful appearance — and it is indeed a bird of extraordinary elegance: grey-bodied with a long white ear-tuft, a black neck and breast, and long decorative tertial feathers that droop over the tail. Demoiselle cranes are rare but annual vagrants to Scandinavia, typically appearing in spring among flocks of common cranes at staging sites such as Lake Hornborga.

children.name: Anthropoides virgo
children.description: An elegant vagrant from the central Asian steppes, seen annually in small numbers among common crane flocks at Hornborga and other Swedish staging sites. Distinctly smaller and more delicate than the common crane, with a black breast, white ear-tufts, and long ornamental tertials. Makes one of the most dramatic migrations of any bird, crossing the Himalayas at extreme altitude to reach Indian wintering grounds. Its name — French for 'young lady' — reflects the grace that enchanted 18th-century European courts.

children.name: Anthropoides paradisea
children.description: The national bird of South Africa, found primarily in the grass- and scrublands of the Cape and Karoo. Its pale blue-grey plumage and long tertial feathers that trail to the ground are unique among cranes. Listed as Vulnerable, with populations declining due to agricultural conversion and poisoning.

children.name: Bugeranus
children.description: The wattled crane — a monotypic genus containing the largest crane in Africa, named for the distinctive, elongated white wattles that dangle from beneath the bill. It is restricted to the undisturbed wetlands and floodplains of Central and Southern Africa and is the most habitat-specialist of all crane species, requiring large, pristine marshes with deep water. Its bugling call is deeper and more resonant than that of any other African crane.

children.name: Bugeranus carunculatus
children.description: Africa's largest crane, standing 175 cm tall with a wingspan of 2.6 m. The white dangling wattles beneath the chin and the greyish-white neck are unmistakable. Found only in large, undisturbed wetlands in southern and east-central Africa. Listed as Vulnerable, with fewer than 8,000 remaining due to habitat loss.
